name: Route of Iron and Coal (Ripoll - Sant Joan de les Abadesses)
name_original: Ruta del Ferro i del Carbó (Ripoll - Sant Joan de les Abadesses)
description:
The Ruta del Ferro y del Carbó (Hierro y el Carbon in Spanish) is a greenway that is the old route of the railway that transported iron and coal from the mines of Ogassa to Ripoll. It is an ideal route to enjoy the nature and culture of this area of the Catalan Pyrenees, on foot or by bike.

The route has a length of 9.5 kilometers, with a gentle slope of 1%. Une Ripoll, at 682 meters of altitude, with Sant Joan de les Abadesses, at 775 meters. Along the way, you can see the historical and cultural heritage of the monastery of Santa Maria de Ripoll or the old town of Sant Joan de les Abadesses.

The "Iron and Coal Route" is a way to get to know the past and the present of this area of Ripollès, and to spend a pleasant day with your family. For more information you can consult the website www.viesverdes.cat
region_intro: Hiking around Ripoll, nestled in the Ripollès region of Catalonia, offers a diverse range of trails within the Catalan Pyrenees. The landscape transitions from riparian forests in valleys to mountain and subalpine forests, eventually reaching alpine meadows and glacial cirques. This region is characterized by its varied terrain, including significant peaks and an extensive network of historic paths. Best hiking trails around Ripoll The most popular hiking route is Ruins of Puig del Catllar loop from Ripoll, a 6.0 miles (9.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 11 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of ancient ruins and the surrounding landscape. Another top favourite among local hikers is Petit Colomer Gorge – Gorg de l'Olla loop from Ripoll, a difficult 15.8 miles (25.5 km) path. This trail explores dramatic gorges and natural water features. Local hikers also love the Parc dels Donants – Chapel with View of Ripoll loop from Ripoll, a 4.0 miles (6.5 km) trail leading through a park and offering panoramic views from a chapel, often completed in about 2 hours. Hiking around Ripoll is defined by its Pyrenean peaks, diverse forest ecosystems, and historic trails, offering options for all ability levels from gentle strolls to challenging ascents. The routes in Ripoll are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 300 reviews. More than 2000 hikers have used komoot to explore Ripoll's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Ripoll?
A: Ripoll offers a wide variety of hiking experiences, with over 50 trails recorded on komoot. These routes cater to all levels, from gentle strolls to challenging mountain ascents, reflecting the region's diverse landscapes and extensive network of paths.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on Ripoll's hiking trails?
A: The terrain around Ripoll is incredibly varied. You can expect to traverse riparian forests in the valleys, ascend through mountain and subalpine forests, and even reach alpine meadows and glacial cirques, especially within the Natural Park of Capçaleres del Ter and Freser. Many trails also follow ancient paths and historic routes.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly hiking options in Ripoll?
A: Yes, Ripoll offers 12 easy routes suitable for families or those seeking a relaxed walk. These trails often provide pleasant, shaded paths, such as sections of the Camí Ral, allowing for enjoyable outings without significant difficulty. Many of these routes are perfect for experiencing the region's natural beauty at a leisurely pace.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in Ripoll?
A: Absolutely. Many of Ripoll's trails are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31866790" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Artigars – Castillo de Milany loop from Llaés&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 13.9 km route that takes you through scenic landscapes and past historical sites.

Q: Are there trails that feature natural water features like gorges or waterfalls?
A: Yes, the Ripollès region is known for its dramatic natural water features. You can explore areas with gorges and natural pools. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40325424" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gorg de l'Olla – Petit Colomer Gorge loop from Campdevànol&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging route that takes you through impressive natural landscapes featuring these water elements.

Q: What historical or cultural points of interest can I explore while hiking in Ripoll?
A: Ripoll is rich in history. Many trails connect significant historical sites, including medieval monasteries like Santa Maria de Ripoll, Sant Joan de les Abadesses, and Sant Pere de Camprodon. You can also find routes that incorporate ancient paths and the "Ruta del Ferro" (Iron Route), showcasing the region's heritage. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2261304" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bellmunt Sanctuary&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2947681" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of Mare de Déu dels Munts&lt;/a&gt; are also notable religious buildings accessible via some routes.

Q: Are there any trails that lead to mountain peaks or offer panoramic views?
A: The Ripollès region is home to several emblematic peaks offering breathtaking views. While some require challenging ascents, many trails provide stunning vistas. Notable peaks in the area include Puigmal, Bastiments, Gra de Fajol, Costabona, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2097175" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Taga Summit (2,040 m)&lt;/a&gt;. Even moderate routes often lead to viewpoints, such as the one from the chapel on the Parc dels Donants loop.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy the most about hiking in Ripoll?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 300 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, from lush forests to high mountain terrain, and the extensive network of well-maintained trails. The combination of natural beauty and historical sites is also frequently highlighted as a major draw.

Q: Is it possible to access hiking trails around Ripoll using public transport?
A: Ripoll itself is accessible by public transport, and some trailheads are within walking distance of the town center. For routes further afield, particularly those in more remote mountain areas, public transport options might be limited. However, the Vall de Núria area, a popular hiking destination, is uniquely accessible by rack railway, offering a convenient way to reach higher elevations.

Q: Are there any huts or refuges available along the hiking routes in Ripoll?
A: Yes, for longer or multi-day hikes, there are huts and refuges available in the Ripollès region. These can provide shelter and sometimes food for hikers. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5532792" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sant Julià de Cabrera Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3812917" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pla de Prats Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5041074" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Covil Shelter&lt;/a&gt;, which are popular stops for those exploring the more extensive mountain trails.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Ripoll?
A: Ripoll offers hiking opportunities year-round, but the best time largely depends on your preferred activity. Spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, ideal for most trails. Summer is excellent for higher-altitude routes in the Natural Park of Capçaleres del Ter and Freser, while winter offers unique snowshoeing or ski mountaineering experiences in the higher peaks, though lower-altitude paths remain accessible.

Q: Are there any specific long-distance or multi-stage hiking tours in the Ripollès region?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers looking for a more immersive adventure, the Ripollès region offers multi-stage journeys. The Hiking Tour of Ripollès, for example, is an eight- or nine-stage journey that allows you to traverse the region's diverse landscapes, connecting various towns and natural highlights over several days.

Unique views of the Sierra del Cadell farmhouse and the entire Sierra Cavallera, with Taga and Sant Amand as relevant peaks, which can be seen from the path that deviates from the greenway, a few meters from the house. Guit.

Pont Vell, also known as Pont de Sant Joan, was originally built in 1138, but during the earthquake of 1428 it suffered considerable damage and was rebuilt and modified in Gothic style, with a spectacular arch, which has one of the largest spans in the world. the city, the Iberian Peninsula.

During the Spanish Civil War it was dynamited to prevent the advance of rebel troops, so it was rebuilt in 1976, respecting the previous reconstruction.

waypoints.name: Views of the Plana del Cadell and Serra de Cavalleria
